results.possibleIdentity: Bert Rundell was a technical supervisor and engineer at Bell Laboratories in the late 1960s and early 1970s who became a key collaborator in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. He was a supervisor in the Television and Acoustics Research Group at Bell Labs and served as a technical consultant for several high-profile art and technology projects. Most notably, Rundell was part of the engineering team for the Pepsi Pavilion at the Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where he supervised the design and installation of the pavilion's complex electronic sound system. This system featured a computer-controlled 37-speaker array that allowed sound to be moved three-dimensionally through the space. In this capacity, he collaborated closely with artist and composer David Tudor to realize the pavilion's immersive acoustic environment. His work exemplifies the E.A.T. mission of fostering direct collaboration between artists and engineers to create new forms of interactive and technological art.

results.possibleIdentity: Victor Hurwitz was a cinematographer, film director, and television director active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is notably recognized as the Director of Photography for Wes Craven's 1972 horror film 'The Last House on the Left'. In the late 1960s, Hurwitz worked as a staff director at WWJ-TV in Detroit. During this time, he served as the director for the pioneering community television project 'In Your Own Interest' (1968), which was a major series aimed at alleviating racial tensions following the 1967 Detroit riots. This project was a significant collaboration involving the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to use technical systems and media for social communication. Hurwitz's work with E.A.T. in Detroit is documented in the organization's historical records. In 1972, he was also involved in a legal dispute (Robert Stigwood Group Ltd. v. Hurwitz) related to his role with January Enterprises Company, which was accused of copyright infringement for promoting unauthorized performances of 'Jesus Christ Superstar'.
